title: The Effect of Dispersion on the Informativeness of Consensus Analyst Target Prices
abstract: Consensus analyst target prices are widely available online at no cost to investors. In this paper, we examine how the amount of dispersion in the individual target prices comprising the consensus affects the predictive association between the consensus target price and future returns. We find that returns implied by consensus target prices and realized future returns are positively correlated when dispersion is low, but they become highly negatively correlated when dispersion is high. Further analyses suggest that the differing effect of dispersion stems from incentive-driven staleness in price targets by some analysts after bad news. As a stock performs poorly and some analysts are slow to update their target prices, dispersion increases, and the consensus target price becomes too high. This has important implications for how consensus analyst target prices should inform investment decisions. We show that a hedge strategy taking a long (short) position in stocks with the highest predicted returns among stocks with the lowest (highest) dispersion earns more than 11% annually. Finally, we show that the negative correlation between consensus-based predicted returns and future realized returns for high-dispersion stocks exists mainly for stocks with high retail interest, suggesting that unsophisticated investors are misled by inflated target prices that are available freely online.

article.1. Introduction: Publicly traded stocks with significant market capitalization typically receive dedicated coverage from multiple financial analysts who each provide periodic research reports and valuation estimates after considering various sources of information about the company. The target price is one of the primary components of an analyst’s research report, and this price target is notable because it is straightforward and easy to understand (Bradshaw et al. 2016). Any investor, even an unsophisticated one, can simply compare the stock’s current price with a future price target to calculate its predicted return (e.g.,Bilinski et al. 2013,Ho et al. 2018).1Prior research has produced mixed evidence on the predictive value of target prices for future realized stock prices, finding that target price levels and revisions are somewhat informative but that a large proportion of stocks do not reach their price targets (e.g.,Dimson and Marsh 1984,Brown et al. 1991,Brav and Lehavy 2003,Asquith et al. 2005,Bradshaw et al. 2013,Joos et al. 2016). This apparent lack of predictive power is surprising in light of the large number of analysts devoted to generating target prices as one of their primary outputs. In this paper, we consider the potential moderating role played by an important but underexplored feature of a collection of individual analysts’ price targets: their dispersion. Conceptually, there are two primary reasons why the observed dispersion in analysts’ target prices might affect their usefulness for predicting future realized stock returns. Most evidently, dispersion may represent genuine differences in opinion among analysts about the expected value of the firm; we refer to this type of dispersion as “disagreement based.” However, dispersion could also develop as analysts respond differently to their implicit incentives to avoid issuing negative opinions about a firm while providing information to clients, and some analysts may (consciously or unconsciously) be slow or partial in updating their target prices (e.g.,McNichols and O’Brien 1997,Bradshaw 2002,Diether et al. 2002,Bonini et al. 2010); we refer to this type of dispersion as “incentive driven.”2 When both types of dispersion are low, predicted returns ought to be more informative about future returns.3When dispersion is purely disagreement based, low dispersion among analysts’ target prices would suggest more certain analysts and more accurate predicted returns (Malkiel 1982,Fisher and Raman 1996,Barron and Stuerke 1998,Guar et al. 2007,Barron et al. 2009,Gaba et al. 2019,Engelberg et al. 2020). When dispersion is purely incentive driven, low dispersion would suggest timelier and less-biased target prices (Diether et al. 2002). Either way, low target price dispersion suggests that analysts’ consensus price targets should be more predictive of future stock prices (i.e., predicted returns should be more positively correlated with future returns for these stocks). Conversely, high dispersion could impair the informativeness of consensus target prices. High disagreement-based dispersion would suggest that analysts are more uncertain about the value of the firm, meaning that consensus target prices are noisier, less-informative signals of the future stock price (i.e., the correlation between predicted returns and future realized returns would be closer to zero for stocks with high disagreement-based dispersion). On the other hand, incentive-driven staleness in target prices could result in consensus target prices that are overly optimistic and exhibit high dispersion, which in turn, could lead to overpriced stocks if investors are attracted to stocks with high target prices (Diether et al. 2002). As a result, the correlation between predicted returns and future returns could be low and might even be negative for stocks with high incentive-driven dispersion. Consistent with these expectations, we find that predicted returns are more positively correlated with future realized returns when dispersion is low (we measure dispersion as the standard deviation of the target prices comprising the consensus scaled by the current stock price). This result suggests that when both disagreement-based dispersion and incentive-driven dispersion are low, target prices are generally more informative about future stock prices, in line with the view that analysts provide value to capital markets. Still, we note that this result tends to hold only for very low-dispersion stocks, meaning that the target price inaccuracies reported in prior research cannot be overcome entirely by conditioning on low dispersion. On the other hand, we find that predicted returns exhibit a strongnegativecorrelation with future returns when dispersion is high. This striking reversal of the predictive correlation between predicted and actual returns suggests that both disagreement-based dispersion and incentive-driven dispersion contribute to the overall weak informativeness of target prices documented in prior literature. Moreover, the economic magnitude of these effects is large. Utilizing the predictable mispricing patterns from both low- and high-dispersion stocks, a hedge portfolio that takes a long (short) position in the stocks from the highest decile of predicted returns within the lowest (highest) dispersion decile earns over 11% annually. This result holds when using four- and five-factor Fama–French models as well as a regression model framework. The economically significant returns to this hedge strategy are notable given that most stock anomalies have not generated significant hedge returns in recent decades (e.g.,Green et al. 2017). Conceptually, disagreement-based dispersion should only indicate larger expected errors in the consensus target price and would not predict that high-dispersion stocks exhibit a negative correlation between predicted returns and realized future returns. However, incentive-driven dispersion can provide an explanation for the patterns we observe. To understand why the correlation between predicted and actual returns diverges for low- and high-dispersion stocks, we consider analysts’ incentives and their decisions about whether, when, and how much to update their target prices. For example, when firm fundamentals deteriorate, incentives may lead (at least some) analysts to delay or only partially adjust when revising their target prices, especially as analysts try to balance their access to management and the informational needs of their clients. Moreover, analysts likely differ in the degree to which they exhibit such behavior. All together, these actions would simultaneously increase observed target price dispersion and leave the consensus price target too high. Although the overall pattern of our results points to a larger role for incentive-driven dispersion than disagreement-based dispersion, we consider each channel in turn. To explore the disagreement-based explanation, we construct a current-month consensus predicted return and dispersion in predicted returns using only individual target prices issued in montht(as opposed to the Institutional Brokers’ Estimate System (IBES) constructed consensus employed in our main tests). By construction, these alternative variables are based on less-stale information than our primary measures, and thus, they are more likely to capture genuine disagreement among analysts. Consistent with both disagreement and incentives playing a role in our main findings, we find that the results based on current-month forecasts are still significant but weaker than our primary results. To explore the incentive-driven explanation, we examine past stock returns and analysts’ revision time across low- and high-dispersion stocks. We find that high-dispersion stocks tend to exhibit very poor recent stock returns, and they tend to be covered by analysts who delay and incorporate less news when updating their target prices, especially for high-dispersion stocks with high predicted returns. In contrast to their slow response to bad news in target prices for high-dispersion stocks, analysts respond more quickly in their earnings forecasts, consistent with the idea that earnings forecasts are much more scrutinized and verifiable than target prices. Taken together, these additional analyses suggest that incentive-driven dispersion is the primary driver of the negative correlation between consensus predicted returns and future returns for high-dispersion stocks. Finally, we consider the implications of our findings for retail investors. Prior research finds that retail investors pay attention to target prices (Lawrence et al. 2017) and tend to take analysts’ opinions at face value without adjusting for analysts’ incentives to publish biased information (Malmendier and Shanthikumar 2007). As a result, we expect retail investors to be more affected by high price targets and to contribute more to the negative association between predicted and realized returns that we document for high-dispersion stocks. From a limits-to-arbitrage perspective, ifretail investorsplace too much confidence in high target prices, the dispersion inall investors’(i.e., not analysts’) expectations about future returns will increase, thereby inflating stock prices, which reflect the opinions of investors with the most positive outlook (Miller 1977,Diether et al. 2002). In other words, if retail investors are ignorant of target price dispersion and naively follow artificially inflated target prices for stocks with high incentive-driven dispersion and if arbitrageurs do not completely arbitrage away the mispricing, the pattern we observe for our main results should be stronger for stocks with more retail interest. Consistent with these arguments, we find that the negative correlation between predicted returns and future realized returns for high-dispersion stocks is driven by stocks with low institutional ownership, high Twitter mentions, and high Robinhood ownership. This result is consistent with the view that retail investors are more likely to be misled by inflated consensus target prices than institutional investors who have access to more data and more information about firms. This paper makes several contributions to the literature. We extend the target price informativeness literature by considering the effects of target price dispersion and the implications of these effects for retail investors. Our evidence that the dispersion of individual analysts’ target prices plays an important moderating role and flips the predictive relationship between consensus target prices and future realized returns for low- versus high-dispersion stocks sheds light on the mixed results from prior research about target price informativeness. Our results also suggest that high target price dispersion can be an indication that analyst incentives are influencing their forecasts. By documenting the effect of dispersion on the informativeness ofconsensustarget prices, we also extend the literature examining the complementary usefulness of various aspects ofindividualanalysts’ target prices, such as scenario-based valuations (Joos et al. 2016,Joos and Piotroski 2017) or their valuation techniques (Gleason et al. 2013). Our findings are distinct from existing results about dispersion and stock returns. Prior studies (e.g.,Diether et al. 2002) find that dispersion is negatively correlated with future returns and conclude that stock prices reflect the views of the most optimistic investors when pessimistic investors do not trade. These papers study the main effect of dispersion, showing that dispersion is negatively correlated with future stock returns. In contrast, we focus on theinteractioneffect of dispersion with predicted returns based on consensus target prices and show that the correlation between predicted returns and future realized returns flips sign for low- versus high-dispersion stocks. Moreover, our findings extend the target price literature by providing a more complete understanding of the information content of target prices and their implications for future returns. In addition to academic researchers, our results are likely to be of interest to practitioners, regulators, and retail investors who rely on target price information. Practitioners will be interested in our findings because they provide a road map for portfolio management based on consensus target prices. Regulators, meanwhile, may find it concerning that retail investors appear to be fooled by optimistic target prices when dispersion is high and may want to encourage the disclosure of more information about the dispersion and staleness of consensus target prices provided freely online. In turn, retail investors who use consensus target prices when making investment decisions will want to be mindful that implied returns can be misleading when dispersion is high. The rest of the paper is organized as follows.Section 2discusses related literature and our research question,Section 3describes the sample data,Section 4presents our research design and main empirical findings,Section 5explores potential explanations for the main results,Section 6considers implications for retail investors, andSection 7concludes.
article.2. Background and Research Question: 2.1. Institutional Background Along with earnings forecasts and stock recommendation, the target price is one of the primary components of an analyst’s research report (Asquith et al. 2005;Bradshaw et al. 2013,2016). In practice, the vast majority of target prices have a 12-month horizon and represent the analyst’s opinion about the stock price 12 months in the future.4AsBrav and Lehavy (2003)note, “Target prices provide market participants with analysts’ most concise and explicit statement on the magnitude of the firm’s expected value” (Brav and Lehavy 2003, p. 1933). For the last 20 years, it has also been common for sell-side analysts’ reports to include target prices in support of their stock recommendations (Bradshaw 2002,Brav and Lehavy 2003,Gleason et al. 2013).Schipper (1991, p. 106) calls stock recommendations “the ultimate analyst judgment,” and target prices are critical because they form the basis of these recommendations (Bradshaw 2004,Gleason et al. 2013). The fact that target price disclosure is regulated in Financial Industry Regulatory Authority Rule 2241 (and its predecessors National Association of Securities Dealers Rule 2711 and New York Stock Exchange Rule 472) provides additional support for the view that target prices are important in capital markets. However, analysts’ incentives should not be ignored when contemplating their disclosed target prices. Although much analyst-focused accounting research tends to focus on quantitative accuracy (for commentary, seeSchipper 1991,Bradshaw 2011, andBradshaw et al. 2016), it is unclear whether analysts (or their employers) care very much about forecast accuracy (e.g.,Francis and Philbrick 1993,Bradshaw 2011,Groysberg et al. 2011,Brown et al. 2015). For example, consider the bank research director quoted inGroysberg et al. (2011)who stated, “I don’t think [forecast accuracy] is any kind of acid test for whether an analyst has keen insight. If the clients pay attention to and pay for the services of an analyst, then that is a ‘good’ analyst, whether or not they get the earnings, or for that matter, stock prices, right” (Groysberg et al. 2011, p. 985).5Moreover, the literature has identified many potential conflicts of interest relating to analysts. For example,Bradshaw (2011)lists incentives related to investment banking fees, currying favor with management, trade generation, institutional investor relationships, research for hire, and analysts’ own behavioral biases as potential concerns when considering analysts’ research, forecasts, and recommendations (also seeChiu et al. 2021). These types of conflicts have been discussed as potential drivers of the positively skewed distribution of stock recommendations (e.g.,Bradshaw 2002), which suggests that they likely also play a role in analysts’ target prices. Although prior studies often useindividualanalyst target prices (and their revisions) to examine analyst incentives and forecast properties, our focus on the dispersion of target prices motivates the use of consensus target prices in our informativeness tests. In addition to this empirical expediency, our focus on dispersion vis-à-vis consensus target prices is important for several reasons. First, because the consensus target price represents the aggregate opinion of multiple analysts, the “wisdom of crowds” (Surowiecki 2005) suggests that it will generally be more accurate than an individual target price, which may increase the credibility of consensus information in the minds of investors.6Second, unlike individual analysts’ price targets, consensus target price information is commonly provided by financial websites to the investing public at no cost. Although consensus target prices are not the only analyst-generated forecasts available online, they are perhaps the easiest to interPRET.7Third, although underlying dispersion is an important characteristic of consensus target prices, detailed information about dispersion is not readily available on financial websites.8Collectively, these factors suggest that it is important to understand the informativeness of consensus target prices because they constitute one of the analyst industry’s primary and most widely accessible outputs. Moreover, our study of the dispersion underlying consensus price targets carries important implications for investors and the purveyors of financial information.9 2.2. Prior Literature and Research Question Prior research generally supports the view that target price levels and revisions carry some information, even though target prices tend to be positively biased, and a large proportion of stocks do not reach their target prices (e.g.,Dimson and Marsh 1984,Brown et al. 1991,Brav and Lehavy 2003,Asquith et al. 2005,Bonini et al. 2010,Bradshaw et al. 2013,Joos et al. 2016). Analysts’ target prices and recommendations are related to their forecasts of earnings and long-term growth (e.g.,Bandyopadhyay et al. 1995,Bradshaw 2004), and analysts’ target price revisions are influenced by recent market performance, stock returns, and other analysts’ revisions (Ho et al. 2018). Target price accuracy is associated with analyst characteristics, valuation techniques and scenarios, earnings forecast accuracy, firm-specific factors, and aspects of country culture (Bilinski et al. 2013,Gleason et al. 2013,Imam et al. 2013,Joos et al. 2016,Joos and Piotroski 2017). However, it appears that the market does not respond differentially to analysts’ target prices based on their historical target price accuracy (Bradshaw et al. 2013). Researchers have also shown that target prices can be more informative when considered in context with other information, such as stock recommendations (Huang et al. 2009), relative rankings within industries (Da and Schaumburg 2011,Da et al. 2016), and the information conveyed by scenario-based valuations (Joos et al. 2016,Joos and Piotroski 2017). Although prior research generally suggests that target prices convey some information, results also show “that target prices are highly inaccurate and biased, and analysts appear to have little skill in forecasting future stock prices” (Dechow and You 2020, p. 127; also seeDimson and Marsh 1984andBradshaw et al. 2013). Moreover, as mentioned, analysts may not care too much about forecast accuracy (e.g.,Groysberg et al. 2011,Brown et al. 2015).10Given these conflicting views and the generally inconclusive evidence about the usefulness of target prices, we argue that one way to improve understanding of target price informativeness is to consider potential moderating factors, particularly those that could provide insight about how particular types of investors might be using analysts’ target prices. To this end, we consider the research question of whether, when, and to what extent the dispersion in individual price targets used to construct the consensus affects the informativeness of that consensus target price. As described inSection 1, we choose to focus on dispersion because it likely has implications for the usefulness of consensus target prices in predicting future returns.11However, it is ex ante unclear what information is conveyed by dispersion in the target price setting. For example, dispersion may arise because of genuine disagreement about firm value among analysts—perhaps because of differences in their private information (i.e., disagreement-based dispersion). Alternatively, dispersion may reflect discrepancies arising from individual analysts responding differently to the various incentives mentioned previously (i.e., incentive-driven dispersion). When observed dispersion is small, both disagreement and incentive-driven differences tend to be low, suggesting that consensus target prices should be more informative. This reasoning is consistent with both analytical (Gaba et al. 2019) and empirical (Malkiel 1982,Fisher and Raman 1996,Barron and Stuerke 1998,Guar et al. 2007,Barron et al. 2009,Engelberg et al. 2020) analyses, suggesting that the dispersion of a set of individual estimates is negatively related to the accuracy of the consensus estimate. However, it is important to note that incentive-driven biases can still exist if observed dispersion is low. If all analysts respond to the same incentives in a similarly biased fashion, then a low-dispersion consensus target price could still be plagued by biases even if genuine disagreement is low. In this case, low dispersion might not result in better consensus target price informativeness. Similarly, the implications of high dispersion for target price informativeness can differ depending on whether disagreement-based dispersion or incentive-driven dispersion dominates. High disagreement-based dispersion stems from genuine uncertainty about firm value among analysts; as a result, consensus target prices characterized by high disagreement might simply be less accurate and less useful for predicting future returns. High incentive-driven dispersion arises when differing objectives lead some analysts to avoid or delay incorporating negative news in their price targets. As a result, their target prices would become more dispersed and optimistically biased. In these cases, stocks with high incentive-driven dispersion and high consensus target prices would be expected to experience lower future returns. Ultimately, the manner in which dispersion impacts the informativeness of consensus target prices is an empirical question. Studying the effects of dispersion provides a better understanding of how an important but underemphasized feature of analysts’ price targets potentially moderates target price informativeness.
article.3. Sample Data and Descriptive Statistic: The data for our main sample come from three sources: (1) analysts’ target prices, analysts’ earnings forecasts, and firms’ actual earnings data from IBES; (2) accruals and other financial variables from annual Compustat files; and (3) stock return data from monthly (and daily) Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP) files. Target prices have only been widely available on IBES starting in 1999; our sample covers the period from July 1999 to December 2020. Because IBES releases consensus target price statistics once per month (usually on the third Thursday), our observations occur at the firm-month level. For most analyses, we simply use the consensus target price provided by IBES (rather than constructing our own consensus from individual forecasts) because it closely matches the consensus target price information available online to investors; this choice also facilitates replication by future researchers.12 Our main dependent variable isRETt+1,t+12, the 12-month future realized return from montht+ 1 to montht+ 12, wheretrefers to the month in which IBES released the consensus target price. Our two primary explanatory variables are the 12-month predicted stock return based on the IBES consensus analyst target price (PRET) and the dispersion in target prices comprising the consensus (DISP).PRETis defined as the average target price in monthtminus stock price in monthtscaled by stock price in montht.13DISPis measured as the standard deviation from the IBES summary target price data for monthtscaled by stock price in montht.14During our analysis, we observed that IBES has some issues with stock split adjustments in target prices. As a precaution, we drop firm-month observations in which the mean target price is different from the median target price by more than 50%. We also follow the finance literature and drop observations with stock prices below $5.15Our main sample includes 537,519 firm-month observations with nonmissingRETt+1,t+12,PRET, andDISP. Table 1presents descriptive statistics and correlations for our three primary variables of interest (RETt+1,t+12,PRET, andDISP) along with several other control variables used throughout the analyses. The variableANrecords the number of analysts contributing to the consensus target price calculation. Market value of equity (MV), the book-to-market ratio (BM), past realized returns from monthst− 6 tot− 1 (RETt−6,t−1), and accruals from the fiscal year (FY) prior to montht(ACC) are commonly used variables that potentially explain stock returns. Given that recommendations are coarser than target prices, it is not unexpected to see recommendations being updated less frequently than target prices. However, the fact that target prices tend to be updated fewer than three times per year suggests that many consensus target prices could be stale, an issue we address inSection 5in the context of incentive-driven dispersion. The findings indicate that, on average, stocks with the highest predicted returns based on consensus target prices tend to perform the worst. Overall, the results from panel D ofTable 1suggest that consensus target prices are generally not predictive of future stock returns because of their inability to meaningfully distinguish future performance among the majority of stocks (PRET1throughPRET8) and the counterintuitive finding that stocks with the highest predicted returns based on the consensus target price (PRET9andPRET10) have the worst future realized returns.
article.4. Main Results: 4.1. The Effect of Dispersion on the Informativeness of Consensus Target Prices: Portfolio Approach We first use the portfolio approach to examine the potential moderating role ofDISPon the association betweenPRETandRETt+1,t+12. Each month, we first sort stocks into deciles based onDISP. Then, within eachDISPdecile, we further sort stocks into deciles based onPRET. The reason we first sort byDISPis that irrespective of the value ofPRET, smaller (larger) values ofDISPmay indicate that the consensus-based predicted return is affected less (more) by disagreement and/or analysts’ incentives. As explained previously, low versus high dispersion suggests possible differences in target price informativeness for future returns; thus, our goal is to first condition on dispersion and then examine informativeness (i.e., the association betweenPRETandRETt+1,t+12) among stocks with similar dispersion. This sorting procedure yields 100 portfolios each month, with each monthly portfolio containing about 20.83 firms on average. For each of our 258 sample months (July 1999 to December 2020), we take the average realized future stock return (RETt+1,t+12) for each portfolio.Table 2presents the time-series average for each portfolio, meaning that each of the 100 portfolio averages is composed of about 5,375 (i.e., 20.83 × 258) firm-month observations. Each month, we first sort stocks into 10 deciles byDISP. Then, for each resultingDISPdecile, we further sort stocks into 10 groups byPRET. The tabulated portfolio returns are the average ofRETt+1,t+12for each portfolio over time; Fama–MacBetht-statistics are in parentheses. The most striking result inTable 2emerges from comparing the stocks with the lowest target price dispersion (DISP1) with those with the highest target price dispersion (DISP10). Within theDISP1decile, realized future returns tend to increase when moving fromPRET1(9.93%) toPRET10(12.89%). As shown in the far-right column inTable 2, a hedge portfolio that goes long inPRET10stocks and short inPRET1stocks within theDISP1decile earns a return of 2.96% (t= 2.54). In contrast to the overall pattern observed in panel D ofTable 1,Table 2shows a positive correlation betweenPRETandRETt+1,t+12when analysts agree about their target prices (i.e., whenDISPis lowest). This finding is consistent with consensus target prices being more useful for predicting future stock returns when the consensus suffers less from disagreement-based or incentive-driven dispersion.18Nevertheless, we note that this positive correlation only manifests when dispersion is very low (e.g., theDISP1andDISP2deciles), consistent with the overall lack of target price informativeness found in prior studies. This result suggests that although conditioning on low dispersion can be useful, it is not a panacea for overcoming target price inaccuracy. At the other end of the spectrum, we observe a drastically different pattern within theDISP10decile. Within this decile, realized future returnsdecreasefrom 13.31% inPRET1to −1.87% inPRET10. In other words, among stocks with the highest dispersion, those with the highest predicted returns performfar worsethan stocks with the lowest predicted returns. Taken together, the results fromTables 2–4suggest that the informativeness of consensus analyst target prices is moderated strongly by the dispersion of individual target prices comprising the consensus. When dispersion is low (i.e., lowerDISP), predicted stock returns derived from the consensus target price are more positively correlated with future returns. This result is consistent with our expectations, but it appears to be concentrated in stocks with very low dispersion and is not consistently strong across methods. On the other hand, however, when dispersion is high (i.e., higherDISP), the relation flips, with predicted stock returns derived from the consensus target price becomingnegativelycorrelated with future stock returns. This strong, robust result suggests that disagreement among analysts—especially incentive-driven dispersion—impairs the informativeness of target prices to such an extent that for high-dispersion stocks, the highest target prices predict the worst future returns. 4.4. In other words, we check whether the patterns in future returns shown in our main results are supported by corresponding patterns in future fundamental performance. Incentive-Driven Analyst Behavior and: Thus far, our results have identified a significant, sizeable, and robust pattern of correlations between predicted returns and future realized returns that differs for low- and high-dispersion stocks. To better understand why these patterns emerge, we next present results from additional tests that focus on analyst behavior and potential staleness in target prices. 5.1. Disagreement-Driven vs. Incentive-Based Dispersion The main findings inTables 2–4show that high dispersion significantly impacts the informativeness of target prices, but ourDISPvariable does not distinguish between disagreement-based and incentive-driven dispersion. Given the results inTable 6and that we find a strong negative association (i.e., in contrast with no association) between predicted and future returns for high-dispersion stocks in our main results, incentive-driven dispersion likely plays a larger role, but it is important to better understand the impacts of both types of dispersion as we consider the extent to which analyst incentive-driven behavior helps explain our main results. We first test whether dispersion is simply capturing genuine analyst disagreement (e.g.,Diether et al. 2002,Li et al. 2021), which would lead to noisier, less useful price targets. To remove (at least some of) the effects of incentive-based dispersion, we construct alternative versions of the predicted return and the accompanying dispersion. Instead of using the consensus numbers calculated by IBES in montht, we construct our own consensus and dispersion figures using only individual target prices issued in montht, which we call current-month predicted returns (CPRET) and current-month dispersion (CDISP). The logic behind this approach is that analysts might not update stale forecasts after bad news because of their incentives to curry favor with managers, generate trading commissions, and preserve institutional relationships. As a result, very recently issued price targets are more likely to reflect the latest information, even if firms have recently announced bad news. In our view, dispersion across a collection of recently issued price targets is more likely to reflect disagreement than differences stemming from incentive-driven biases.25 We can then use the constructed measuresCPRETandCDISPin our regression framework fromEquation (2). This approach allows us to see what happens whenPRETandDISPare replaced byCPRETandCDISPand to also estimate the regression when both versions of the variables are included simultaneously. Confirming that our main results still hold in this smaller sample, column (1) inTable 7shows thatPRET×DISPcontinues to load significantly negative, with a coefficient magnitude similar to the first column inTable 4(thet-statistic is smaller but still significant atp< 0.01). Analyst Incentives and the Decision to Update Target Prices Next, we consider how the effects of analysts’ incentives might manifest in observable characteristics of their target prices. In practice, analysts may face competing incentives that, either consciously or unconsciously, can affect their decisions of whether and how much to revise their price targets for different companies. One obvious objective is accuracy; ceteris paribus, analysts would like to serve their clients by providing a price target that closely matches the realized stock price one year later. To support this objective, analysts should update their price targets as new information about the stock emerges. However, analysts can also feel tacit pressure to cater to management and to generate trading commissions and investment banking business by issuing optimistic target prices and focusing on stocks with significant investor interest. For example, an analyst who stays in the good graces of the firm being covered may be rewarded with increased access to management; similarly, an analyst focusing on stocks with more market interest likely generates additional investment banking business and trading commissions (e.g.,Bradshaw 2011,Bradshaw et al. 2016,Chiu et al. 2021). This second objective gives analysts an incentive to issue higher target prices and to quickly revise the target price upward after good news about the stock. However, when bad news about the stock emerges or when market interest wanes, analysts’ two objectives may come into conflict. If bad news or other factors lead to less stock market interest (i.e., lower trading volumes and commissions), analysts may have weaker incentives to update target prices, and they could also be hesitant to revise target prices downward for fear of upsetting management. Yet, analysts might also grow concerned if stock prices drift too far away from their price targets. These considerations may affect an analyst’s decision about whether, how much, and how quickly to revise the target price. For these reasons, differences across analysts and stocks in the staleness and optimism of target prices may provide insight about the degree to which predicted returns and observed dispersion are affected by analyst incentives. To investigate this possibility, we examine individual analyst target prices for a particular stock and how they relate to other target price-updating decisions—either for the same stock by other analysts or by the same analyst for other stocks. First, we consider the possibility that some analysts update their target prices for a particular stock, whereas others do not by estimating the following model: InEquation (4),ROPTijtis the relative optimism of analystj’s target price measured as the natural log of the analyst’s target price scaled by the median target price for firmiin montht.REVTIMEijtis the number of days between analystj’s last posted target price (prior to montht) for firmiand the target price in montht(scaled by a factor of 10,000 for expositional purposes). The inclusion ofFIRM × TIMEfixed effects absorbs all firm characteristics for a given firm-month, ensuring that we can explore variation in relative optimism (ROPT) among analysts following each firm at a specific point in time. We take this approach because some analysts may have stronger incentives than others to issue optimistic target prices. Relatedly, analysts facing similar incentives may respond differently. If target price staleness and optimism are indeed influenced by incentives, we would expect that analysts who are slower in updating their target prices (i.e., higherREVTIME) after bad news versus good news tend to be those with more optimistic target prices (i.e., higherROPT). In the context ofEquation (4), we would expect theREVTIMEcoefficient to be more (less) positive for stocks with worse (better) recent performance. Panel A ofTable 8reports estimations ofEquation (4)performed separately for each quartile of recent performance (measured as the stock return over the prior three months,RETt−2,t). The results are consistent with our expectations, with theREVTIMEcoefficient decreasing monotonically across quartiles ofRETt−2,tfrom 1.049 (t= 14.06) in the most negative quartile of prior returns to −0.161 (t= −3.19) in the top quartile. It is striking that theREVTIMEcoefficient is not only less positive but also, becomes significantly negative for the top quartile of prior returns. These findings suggest that for stocks with recent bad news (good news), analysts with more optimistic target prices in the current month are those who have been slower (faster) to update their target prices, suggesting that target price optimism after bad news is closely tied to incentive-induced staleness in target prices. Next, we consider average target price revision time (REVTIME). Although average target price revision time declines slightly from 132 days inPRET1to 129 days inPRET10for the lowest-dispersion stocks, itincreasesfrom 133 days inPRET1to 156 days inPRET10for the highest-dispersion stocks. In other words, the high-DISP, high-PRETstocks are those with the longest revision times, implying that these stocks have more stale consensus target prices and again, suggesting that incentive-driven staleness is an important factor underlying the high observed dispersion for these stocks, especially because panel A ofTable 9shows that these stocks also have the worst recent performance. The next variable in panel B ofTable 9isTPREVt−3,t, which captures the change in the mean analyst target price from montht− 3 to monthtscaled by stock price in montht.TPREVt−3,ttends to decline fromPRET1toPRET10for high-dispersion stocks, but the magnitudes of the differences fromPRET1toPRET10are smaller than those observed for past stock returns in panel A ofTable 9. It is also noteworthy thatDISP10stocks have much more negative revisions. This suggests that bad news is not being ignored entirely for high-DISP, high-PRETstocks; rather, it is likely that at least some analysts following these stocks are slow in adjusting their price targets after bad news (again, recall that panel A ofTable 9shows that these stocks have the worst prior returns). To provide additional support for this interpretation, we build on the logic underlying the construction ofCPRETandCDISP(seeTable 7) to consider whether stocks exhibiting evidence of analysts ignoring negative information and allowing their price targets to become stale tend to be stocks with high observed dispersion and large predicted returns. More specifically, we definePRET-CPRETas the difference betweenPRETandCPRET, whereCPRETis the current-month predicted return based only on individual target prices issued in montht. AsCPRETreflects more recent information,PRET-CPRETcaptures the staleness in the consensus target prices provided to investors. Notably,PRET-CPRETincreases from −6.0% inPRET1to 52.5% inPRET10for the highest-DISPstocks, whereas it increases only slightly for the lowest-DISPstocks. Taken together, the results in panels A and B ofTable 9suggest that a lack of timely updates to target prices—particularly after bad news—appears to be one key reason for high dispersion to exist in the first place. Moreover, this lack of timely updates also helps explain why high predicted returns are negatively associated with future returns when the incentive-driven dispersion is large. If high dispersion is a consequence of stale information and if these stocks also tend to have the worst recent performance, it suggests that staleness also leads to consensus target prices being simply too high.30 Finally, we compare analysts’ different outputs (e.g., stock recommendations versus forecasts) to yield insight into their strategic, incentive-driven behavior (Malmendier and Shanthikumar 2014). Specifically, we consider earnings forecast staleness as a counterfactual to shed more light on the incentives underlying analysts’ target price behavior. One primary difference between target prices and earnings forecasts is that earnings forecasts are regularly benchmarked against actual earnings when firms announce quarterly results. In contrast, target prices always forecast the stock price 12 months ahead; there is no “announcement date” when target prices are evaluated. As a result, analysts may reasonably view earnings forecast accuracy (which is regularly and consistently evaluated) as more important than target price accuracy (which tends to be ignored). In addition, prior literature shows that consistent with managers’ preferences, long-horizon (short-horizon) earnings forecasts tend to be more (less) optimistic and that analysts are quicker to incorporate bad news for shorter forecast horizons (e.g.,Berger et al. 2019,Ham et al. 2022). Because target prices have longer horizons, it follows that analysts would be more willing to ignore bad news in the context of target prices. Panel C ofTable 9presents corresponding measures for annual earnings forecasts across our sample portfolios (DEt−3,t,EREVTIME,EREV, andEFC-CEFC) to those presented in panel B ofTable 9for target prices. In addition, we includeMEETBEAT(the percentage of firms just meeting or beating quarterly earnings forecasts in each portfolio) in the final row of panel C ofTable 9. In contrast to the sharp increase in target price staleness fromPRET1toPRET10for high-DISPstocks (panel B ofTable 9), we observe less difference in earnings forecast staleness fromPRET1toPRET10for high-DISPstocks in panel C ofTable 9. These results suggest that target price staleness is not driven by analysts dropping coverage or misinterpreting bad news but that it is instead driven by analysts’ incentives. Overall, the evidence inTables 7–9suggests that analysts may not incorporate all public information into their price targets, particularly when it is negative, possibly because of their incentives and/or conflicts of interest (e.g.,McNichols and O’Brien 1997,Hayes 1998,Raedy et al. 2006).31As a result, predicted returns from consensus target prices will be inflated for high-dispersion stocks.
article.6. Implications for Retail Investors: So far, our analyses have shown that dispersion in analysts’ target prices significantly moderates the informativeness of consensus target prices. More specifically, we have shown that an investor can earn significant returns by going long in stocks with lowDISPand highPRETwhile shorting stocks with highDISPand highPRET. This strategy obviously requires access to the cross-section of predicted returns along with the underlying target price dispersion; moreover, only investors who appreciate the potential impact of analysts’ incentives on target prices and their dispersion might think to consider such a strategy. These requirements are important because they are unlikely to be satisfied by unsophisticated retail investors, yet these are precisely the sort of investors who are most likely to be swayed by high consensus target prices that are available freely online.Lawrence et al. (2017)find that retail investors on Yahoo! Finance typically pay more attention to target prices and ratings than earnings forecasts; they also appear to pay much less attention to detailed financial statements and U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission filings than analyst-provided information. Relatedly,Malmendier and Shanthikumar (2007)argue that small investors tend to follow analyst advice literally instead of adjusting for analysts’ incentives. As a result, we expect that retail investors are more affected by inflated consensus target prices and that these investors are an important driver of the observed negative relationship between predicted and future realized returns for high-dispersion stocks. The basic idea underlying this prediction is that because consensus target prices are freely available, are easy to interpret, and have the guise of credibility, retail investors will simply place too much weight on them, leading them to buy stocks with high predicted returns based on consensus target prices. Prior research supports this intuition, withBarber and Odean (2008)asserting that retail investors are “net buyers of attention-grabbing stocks” (Barber and Odean 2008, p. 785) and withBarber and Odean (2000)contending that retail investors often experience poor investment performance because of overconfidence in their investment decisions. From a limits-to-arbitrage perspective, retail investors relying too much on high consensus target prices increases the dispersion ininvestors’(not analysts’) expectations about future returns, thereby inflating stock prices, which reflect the opinions of investors with the most positive outlook (Miller 1977,Diether et al. 2002). Stated differently, it is possible that if retail investors naively follow inflated target prices for high-dispersion stocks and arbitrageurs do not completely arbitrage away the mispricing, the pattern we observe for our main results could be exacerbated for stocks with more retail interest.32 To test this idea, we partition our sample into two groups in each month based on retail interest, re-estimateEquation (2), and compare thePRET×DISPcoefficient between the groups. We use three different proxies for retail interest to ensure the robustness of our inferences:RETAIL, which is one minus the percentage of institutional owners;TWEETS, which is the number of tweets referencing the company’s “cash tag” on Twitter; andROBINUSERS, which is the average number of Robinhood users who own the stock. If retail investors are attracted to stocks with high consensus target prices and if they play a significant role in the patterns observed in our main results, we expect thePRET×DISPcoefficient to have a significantly larger (negative) magnitude in the high-RETAIL, high-TWEETS, and high-ROBINUSERSsubsamples. Table 10reports the results ofEquation (2)after we partition the sample based on the three proxies for retail interest described. Across all measures of retail interest, we find that thePRET×DISPcoefficient is more negative in the high-retail subsamples (t-statistics range from −2.24 to −8.79 in these subsamples). Moreover, the difference in thePRET×DISPcoefficient between low- and high-retail subsamples is statistically significant for each retail interest proxy (t-statistics range from 2.20 to 5.67). Notably, our proxies for retail interest are available for different time periods (indicated in the last row ofTable 10), illustrating the robustness of the findings. These results suggest that stocks with high retail interest exhibit the most mispricing for high-PRET, high-DISPstocks, plausibly because retail investors exert buying pressure on stocks with high predicted returns based on consensus target prices. The coefficient estimates are the average of monthly estimates over time; Fama–MacBetht-statistics are in parentheses. There are two main takeaways from these results. First, from a trading strategy perspective, sophisticated investors can likely earn even more returns if they focus on stocks with high retail interest before going long in low-DISPand high-PRETstocks and shorting high-DISPand high-PRETstocks. To test this conjecture, we recalculate the hedge returns fromTables 2and3after partitioning the sample based on retail investor ownership. As expected, untabulated results show that the hedge returns are much larger for high-retail stocks (ranging from 12.07% to 32.47%) than for low-retail stocks (ranging from 5.41% to 8.84%) or for the full sample shown inTables 2and3(ranging from 11.02% to 28.25%). Second, retail investors should exercise caution before buying stocks with high predicted returns based on consensus target prices. As indicated by the correlations betweenDISPandPRETin panel B ofTable 1, these stocks are likely to also exhibit high dispersion, and this dispersion plausibly stems from significant incentive-driven staleness in the consensus (seeSection 5). In light of this fact, online financial platforms could consider disclosing more information about the dispersion and staleness underlying consensus target prices. Having said that, some platforms (e.g., Yahoo! Finance and MarketWatch) already provide the range of analyst target prices, which could potentially be used by retail investors to proxy for underlying staleness-driven dispersion. To test this idea, we defineRANGEas the highest target price in monthtminus the lowest target price in monthtscaled by the current stock price in montht(using IBES data), and we repeat our main portfolio analyses usingRANGEinstead ofDISP. The results, which are presented inTable 11, are very similar to those inTable 2. This similarity suggests that retail investors could use the range of target prices as an alternative to target price dispersion to identify and avoid problematic stocks with high predicted consensus returns and high analyst incentive-driven staleness.33 Table 11.Replication of Main Portfolio Results After ReplacingDISPwithRANGE Table 11.Replication of Main Portfolio Results After ReplacingDISPwithRANGE Notes. This table replicates the main portfolio results fromTable 2when replacingDISPwithRANGE.RANGEcaptures the range of target prices and is calculated as the maximum target price minus the minimum target price scaled by stock price (all from montht).
article.7. Conclusion: Consistent with prior research using individual price targets, we find that predicted returns based on consensus target prices are generally not informative about future returns, and in fact, stocks with the highest predicted returns tend to have the lowest realized future returns. To shed more light on this lack of target price informativeness, we investigate whether, when, and to what extent the dispersion in analysts’ target prices moderates the degree to which consensus-based predicted returns are informative about future stock returns. We show that the dispersion in individual analyst target prices comprising the consensus is a critical moderating factor. When dispersion is low, we observe a positive association between consensus predicted returns and future realized returns. In other words, when individual analysts agree about the target price and when the consensus is less affected by incentive-driven dispersion, the consensus prediction tends to correlate more positively with the future stock price. We note, however, that conditioning on low dispersion is not a cure-all for overcoming the inaccuracies in target prices because the positive association is not consistently strong across our empirical methods. On the other hand, when target price dispersion is high, we observe a strong, robust negative association between consensus-based predicted returns and future realized returns. Follow-up analyses suggest that high-dispersion and high-predicted return stocks tend to exhibit recent poor performance, and some analysts are slow to update their target prices to reflect this information. As a result, consensus target prices are inflated for these stocks. Underscoring the economic significance of this pattern, we show that an investor can earn annual hedge returns above 11% by going long in stocks with low dispersion and high predicted returns while shorting stocks with high dispersion and high predicated returns. We also find that the strong negative correlation between consensus predicted returns and future realized returns manifests mainly for stocks with high retail interest, suggesting that retail investors are simultaneously misled by high consensus price targets and unaware of the implications of high dispersion. Given that consensus target prices are readily available online at no cost but that the dispersion isnotfreely available, our results suggest that retail investors should use considerable caution before buying stocks with high price targets. Moreover, purveyors of financial information should carefully consider which (incomplete) financial information should be made widely available to investors online. From a regulatory perspective, one potential remedy would be to encourage financial websites to disclose the dispersion, staleness, or other information about the makeup of target prices that underlie the consensus.

AItitle: 分析师目标价分歧陷阱：高分散组合对冲策略年化11%收益
AIsummary: 【金融预测中的"分歧陷阱"：当分析师目标价成为反向指标】这篇论文揭开了华尔街分析师目标价的一个有趣悖论：看似权威的共识预测竟可能成为投资陷阱！研究团队发现，当多位分析师目标价高度趋同时（低分歧），其共识确实能预示未来股价走势；但若分析师意见分歧较大（高分歧），结果竟出现戏剧性反转——目标价越乐观的股票，后续表现反而越差！传统研究对分析师目标价的预测价值争论不休，本文创新性地引入"意见分歧"这一调节变量，揭示了两种隐藏机制：低分歧反映真实市场共识，而高分歧往往暗藏分析师的人为拖延——当公司遭遇利空时，部分分析师出于维护客户关系等动机，故意延迟下调目标价，导致共识价虚高形成泡沫。通过构建对冲策略（做多低分歧高预测股/做空高分歧高预测股），研究者实现了年化11%的超额收益。更值得警惕的是，这种"高分歧陷阱"对散户杀伤力最大！数据显示，在散户持股比例高、社交媒体热议的股票中，虚高目标价的误导效应尤为显著。这为普通投资者敲响警钟：免费获取的共识目标价可能正是华尔街留给你的甜蜜毒药。
Highlights:
学术要点 1：激励驱动分散度（Incentive-driven Dispersion） 分析师因利益驱动（如维护客户关系、避免负面评级）导致目标价更新滞后，形成的目标价差异，区别于信息分歧导致的分散。|||
学术要点 2：目标价格陈旧性（Target Price Staleness） 分析师对负面消息反应迟缓，导致部分目标价未及时更新，造成共识目标价虚高的现象。|||
学术要点 3：意见分歧驱动分散度（Disagreement-based Dispersion） 分析师基于私有信息差异形成的目标价离散程度，反映对企业价值判断的不确定性。|||
学术要点 4：零售投资者误导效应（Retail Investor Misguidance） 散户因过度依赖免费共识目标价且忽视分散度指标，导致高分散度高预测回报股票的逆向定价。|||
学术要点 5：目标价范围替代指标（RANGE Proxy） 用目标价最高值与最低值之差替代标准差，作为分散度的有效代理变量，揭示分析师激励偏差。
